So I'm getting back to the pissing in the pool story. So you had mentioned that it illustrates. Yeah, I told it quickly and badly by the way. So my question has to do with illustration. So illustrations, photographs to help to further relay the story. When is it appropriate? When does it get in the way? For example, if you know, I was just thinking about the pissing in the pool story. Would a picture of a young kid sort of pissing in a pool, maybe you see the back of him and the stream going into the pool. I'll tell you what I've seen Vance show. But specifically my question is does it ever get in the way? And so when is it appropriate to use it? When is it inappropriate? Well the first answer, so first of all I think it's appropriate to use it when the person needs help delivering it. So if we're talking about verbal, you know, Sinatra's line was, you know, anybody that needs more in a spotlight and a microphone is a putz. Now he's right, except there aren't a lot of Sinatra's. So like in an environment, like, so I've seen Vance pop up on the PowerPoint's presentation for that story. The photograph of the fountain with the little cherub guy that pisses in the fountain, right? Which everybody's seen that fountain, everybody knows that fountain. He pops that up, it gets a laugh, etc. But I think you're absolutely right, in many cases it can get in the way. In writing I think it can get in the way. Now I think those of us who are real word oriented, we don't use photos and illustrations enough. But it can definitely get in the way, just like my thing about the bullets. See for me, if it interrupts the rhythm of delivering on paper or on stage, that's not good. Because they like, you know how dogs and cats don't know what we're saying, they respond to the tone of our voice, right? So you say the same thing to them in five different tones and get five different reactions. The people sort of respond, even in print, more to the feelings evoked by it than the details. And so if you get in the way of like interrupting the feeling with something, I think that's not good. Now some people like diagrams and charts and shit because they're analytical, and so there's a place and a time to give them that. If I was doing it in a book I'd stick it at the end of the chapter, so it didn't interrupt the rhythm of the story. A cartoon management book, I had Vince do cartoons, but I spent a lot of time thinking about where do I put it so it doesn't screw up what I'm doing. But I do think we underuse it. And I think purely for speakers, you can take, you can help a speaker a lot, who's not going to ever be Sinatra with pictures, cartoons, objects, you know, like Vince says he speaks by artifact. So he lines up 12 things that remind, it's like me with transparencies, that each one of those is a hunk of something to talk about, so he doesn't have to remember his speech, the coffee cup is this thing and the rock is this thing, gives him something to do with his hands, gives people something to look at. I think my a-ha this weekend has been that my past sales and mostly positioning, one-on-ones helped me a lot do this, some of this subconsciously, but I heard you say something yesterday to someone where it wasn't one of the exercises that they were selling too much as opposed to being more influential. And I think I would, you know, for lack of a better word, I'd consider that more of the positioning differential within sales, but where's the line, what was the line that you were trying to explain? Well, usually what the mistakes most people do, especially if they're say, which I have, if we have a sales background, is we tend to jump to the pitch too soon, or we tend to be overly sales-y, you know, and so, again, we're trying to do it through sort of brute force, and we're capable of doing that, and we're capable of doing it in print, by the way, too, so a sales letter, you know, is really a microcosm of what we're talking about here, but always the best result is when the person feels they've figured it out and moved towards you, rather than you came knocking on the door, you know, it's all the way into my sort of reluctance to put a telemarketing step early, rather than late, because I think the long-term value of the customer is always better when they feel like they came to you, rather than you came and got them, you know? And so that's why you use takeaway language, that's, you know, all of that. So the line, I don't know where it is, but I know, like, it's like pornography, you know, you know it when you see it, you know, like I know when somebody's there too quick, and they're trying to, like, jump the gun and get right into the pitch, rather than let the person decide they're real, that they like them, that they connect with them, and then they buy from them. Howard Bonnell, who was the National Sales Manager for World Book for, I don't know, 15, 20 years, I did tape programs for Howard when I had General Cassette. Howard said, he said, the best encyclopedia salesman tell four stories before they ask every trial close. So his rule was, you got to have four stories for every trial close, you got to hold yourself back from the trial close until you've told the four stories. And so that was like his little discipline for everybody. And he said, until you got him there, you know, the presentation didn't work. My honor, I'm going to reinforce something I tested last year. I should have started with my list about my son, who has Down syndrome, and I'm sorry about how he taught me about living in a moment, and peace of mind, and things he taught me, and we're left to send it out to everybody, so it's sort of very personal. And I sent it out to 30,000, 20,000 people, and the response was just overwhelming. The emails, and even this day, you know, it was every day saying, it had to change my life, it had to change my wall, I printed out, I've handed out to my staff, and maybe realized this weekend that I need to do more of that, the story, the connection, and they see me as a whole different person now, but I've shared that story with people. Yeah, well, the more you let them in, and there's an understandable reluctance to do it, but the more you let them in, the more likely they are to connect with you on a deeper level than transactional or value of, core value of it. In most of the, like, your business and my business, like, to keep them in coaching for four years, five years, six years, seven years, ten years, right? They got it all in the first year, right? I mean, you know, my list is a little of what I teach is a little more elaborate than Jim's, but not a lot. It's Halbert's Clicks on the Dial thing. I mean, you know, in a relatively short period of time, you kind of got Dan direct marketing, and you really don't need to hear Dan direct marketing anymore, so why do you stay around? Well, you stay around for other reasons, obviously, you know. And so the more of that you do, the more, you know, it's a big retention issue, you know, is really what it is. Ronald Graham can also tell you how to set up a Roth IRA for that kid and pay him royalties for the use of his story. And I'm only half kidding, by the way. I mean, yeah, actually, you probably ought to do that. Okay, one more, and we're going to tell you my two more. I believe the storytelling is rare enough, still rare enough that whenever someone tells a story, they're automatically given authority, credibility, likability, all which lead to influence. Maybe a lot of you don't have direct competitors, but I have several direct competitors, and I know that none of them are telling stories. So just by the very fact that I will tell a story, even if it's a bad story, it's going to give me more influence than they have. Yeah, I mean, you, well, I'll tell you, it's sort of where there's direct competitors. So when I first started to work with Gold by the Inch, Len was like you. So the direct mail piece that went out to the lead was the full-color catalog of all the different gold displays, right? Price list and a one-page sales letter that basically told you the deal, right? Here we are, and we've been in business for X number and the thing, you buy the shit for this much and you sell it for that much and yada, yada, yada. That's it. I added a 12-page sales letter that was mostly Len's story. How did Gold by the Inch come to be? 49 previously unsuccessful businesses before this thing finally worked. We had a little picture because Len had them on the wall of all 49 business cards from all previously failed 49 businesses. And the story of him, his first outing to the swap meet with his little homemade display of the chains to see if this would work or not. And even though it would, he only came home with $55 that day. I mean, the incredible excitement of actually seeing something work and having somebody come up and give you money, you know, and validating your idea. And so there's like eight stories in a 12-page letter, right? Well, response through the roof, right? And then again, you heard people on the phone telling the story back and I've been in five businesses and failed and so sure. And most of who in your cat, you know, whether they're on the professional side selling product at wholesale to some kind of store that resells it at retail or the bizop side, nobody does it. So we had, we didn't have, he didn't have like a jewelry competitor, but really every bizop so the Cassie put images on cups, you know, they're all our competitors. And I never saw anybody copy it. I'm sure everybody who actually paid any attention to it thought he was a, you know, a goofball. But see, think about, well, I think about a mundane product like what I did with the shed shop business. So there's as mundane as you can get, but, you know, none of the marketing I did for Paul has anything to do with sheds. It's all people telling stories. So it's his story and then it's Susie that's got the one in the backyard for the dollhouse and this one that's got that and that the one that got that. And I'm looking for diversity. And so yeah, in that case, oh, it was like 10 times. Oh, I was the hero of, you know, and I wouldn't always be that dramatic, but in that case, it was like 10 times. Gold by the intro for four. Gold by the, yeah. Oh, the closing went through the rough, the average size order doubled on and on and on and on and on. In a lot of your copy, I noticed that you almost kind of just really pack it. You pack a paragraph of like four or five different illusions. For example, on 228, you say in an insanity hearing when his relatives were trying to have him declared incompetent. So they would grab his fortune. Henry Ford displayed a lack of knowledge about current events in history that rivals out of the empty heads. Leno finds his jaywalking segments. So and then you continue on and then you bring in Aristotle and asses. Glenn Turner. There's almost this kind of piling on of people that they may or may not know often do. Can you speak to that? You talked about mental imprint. Is there a purpose beyond it almost seems like you just have so many illusions that you want to cram them all in there. But I would assume there's more of a deliberate purpose. King of the mixed metaphor. Well, again, think here's one thing to think about. Now go back and read the paragraph again and think rhythm. And that particular paragraph causes you to speed up. There's also the, as you say, people may not know one, but they know some of it. It does deliver one idea. And so it's just reinforcing that idea has many different ways that validated that would be meaningful to people as possible. There's times to be compact and times not to be compact. By the way, the whole thing of rhythm of speeding them up and slowing them down. That's like one of the most interesting things to me at Disney is how they change the music to get to people in and out of the parks. And if there's too many people at this ride in one area of the park, they have music variations they make to cause people to move, they figured all that out. It's incredible. So like at the end of the day, when they want to get your ass out of there after the fireworks, watch how the music changes. It shifts from, it's kind of like right before the Epcot fireworks, it's sort of this lilting, classic musical thing causing people to be patient while they gather and wait for the thing to start. And afterwards, it's peppier and peppier and peppier all the way to the point, it's like marching music. And everybody's like marching together out the door of the park. Well, the Imagineers told us none of that's accidental. You were at the Imagineers meeting, weren't you with my wealth? Yeah, you were there. Didn't you go to my wealth? Yeah, yeah, yeah. I mean none of that's accidental. Right? And so we can do the same thing with pacing in the way we do words. I try to build up to, you know, bring them in slow, build up to a point, and then maybe even back the pace back down to continue up to maybe even a stronger point or later. That pace helps you clarify exactly what you're trying to explain. Yeah, and again, you see more of this with fiction guys than you do with nonfiction guys, because a lot of nonfiction guys keep essentially writing information. But you see control of pace done better by fiction guys. I just want to make a comment. When you were describing Disney, it reminded me of the film Triumph of the Will about the rise of the Nazi party by Lenny Riefenstam. It's an excellent, I recommend it as a surfing example of Disney-like techniques applied in politics.
